E-COMPONENT-021: Component `Card` has a malformed body that could not be re-parsed. Phase 1 supports self-closing and simple open-tag component definitions only. Parse error: unexpected token in expression position: Ellipsis
E-COMPONENT-020: Component `Card` is not defined in this file. Define it with `const Card = <element .../>` before using it, or check the spelling.

E-COMPONENT-035: Component `Card` survived component expansion (CE) but was not resolved. This is a post-CE invariant violation: every markup node resolved to user-component (or unresolved with an uppercase tag) MUST be expanded into HTML markup or rejected with E-COMPONENT-020 at CE time. The residual reference would otherwise be silently emitted as `document.createElement("Card")`, producing a phantom DOM element with no content. Likely cause: cross-file component import is not yet supported in this consumption shape (see F-COMPONENT-001 deep-dive). Workaround: wrap the component call in an HTML element inside a `lift` expression, e.g. `lift
`.

E-CONTROL-FLOW-IN-MARKUP: a bare `for` control-flow statement directly in a markup body is not recognised as logic and would ship as raw text into the DOM. Per §17.4 / §7, control flow in a markup body MUST be wrapped in a `${ ... }` logic block. Canonical iteration: `<ul>${ for (x of @items) { lift <li>${x}</> } }</>` (Tier-0, §17.4); or the Tier-1 `<each in=@items>` form (§17.7). (line 4, col 9)
E-SYNTAX-044: `given` takes bare identifiers, not property paths (§42.2.3). Bind the property to a local variable first: `let n = u.<field>`, then `given n { ... }`. (line 5, col 11)
E-CTRL-005: `else` or `else-if=` and `if=` cannot appear on the same element. (line 5, col 9)
E-CTRL-001: `else` on line 7 has no preceding `if=` element at the same level. (line 7, col 9)
E-EQ-004: `===` is not a valid scrml operator. Use `==` instead — scrml equality is always strict, so `==` is the only form (§45.7).
E-SYNTAX-010: `else` arm must be the last arm in a `match` block (§18.6). Found 1 arm(s) after `else`. Move the `else` arm to the bottom of the match, or remove arms that appear after it.
E-TYPE-023: Duplicate match arm for variant `::Active`. The second arm for `Active` can never be reached. Remove the duplicate arm.
E-SYNTAX-010: `else` arm must be the last arm in a `match` block (§18.6). Found 1 arm(s) after `else`. Move the `else` arm to the bottom of the match, or remove arms that appear after it.
E-MATCH-012: Match on `T | not` type lacks a `not` arm and lacks an `else`/wildcard arm. Add a `not => ...` arm or an `_ => ...` wildcard to handle the absence case (§42).
E-TYPE-006: Non-exhaustive match over union type. Missing members: string. Add arms for the missing types, or add an `else` arm to handle them all.

E-SCOPE-001: Undeclared identifier `n` in logic expression. No variable, function, type, or import with that name is in scope. Check for a typo, a missing `import`, or whether you meant a reactive `@n`.

E-SYNTAX-011: Match arm guard clauses (`| cond` or `if cond`) are not supported in v1 (§18.10). Arm: `. Circle ( r ) if …...`. Use a plain pattern and move the condition into an `if` inside the arm body.
E-TYPE-020: Non-exhaustive match over enum type `Shape`. Missing variants: ::Point. Add arms for the missing variants, or add an `else` arm to handle them all.

E-TYPE-026: `match` is not valid directly in markup (§18.9). Wrap the match in a logic interpolation — e.g. `${ match subject { .A =>

a else =>

b } }` — so the parser recognises it as a statement rather than text content.
E-TYPE-020: Non-exhaustive match over enum type `Status`. Missing variants: ::Pending. Add arms for the missing variants, or add an `else` arm to handle them all.
E-TYPE-025: Cannot match on `asIs`-typed subject. `match` requires a typed subject (enum, union, or primitive). Narrow the type first via a type annotation (`let x: SomeType = ...`) before matching.

E-TYPE-024: Cannot match on struct-typed subject `Point`. `match` supports enums, unions, and primitive literals (§18.8.2). Use field access (`obj.field`) or destructuring in a normal expression instead.
E-MATCH-012: Match on `T | not` type lacks a `not` arm and lacks an `else`/wildcard arm. Add a `not => ...` arm or an `_ => ...` wildcard to handle the absence case (§42).
E-TYPE-006: Non-exhaustive match over union type. Missing members: string. Add arms for the missing types, or add an `else` arm to handle them all.

E-SCOPE-001: Undeclared identifier `partial` in logic expression. No variable, function, type, or import with that name is in scope. Check for a typo, a missing `import`, or whether you meant a reactive `@partial`.

E-MATCH-ARM-MARKUP-IN-VALUE: a JS-style `match` arm returns a VALUE, not markup (§18.0) — but this arm body is a markup element. Use a `<match for=Type [on=expr]>` block (the structural Tier-1 form, §18.0.1) to render a UI tree per variant, or fire a variant's `renders` display via the render-expression. To compute a VALUE per variant (a string / number), have the arm return that value (e.g. `:> "Failed: " + reason`) and interpolate it in markup.
E-TYPE-020: Non-exhaustive match over enum type `Status`. Missing variants: ::Inactive. Add arms for the missing variants, or add an `else` arm to handle them all.

E-SWITCH-FORBIDDEN: `switch` is not a scrml keyword. Did you mean: `<match for=Type> ... </match>` for structural exhaustive case-analysis (Tier 1 block form; produces markup or executes statements per arm), or `match expr { .Variant -> ... }` for value-return case-analysis (Tier 1 JS-style form; produces a value in expression position)? See SPEC §18 for match block-form, primer §1 for the tier ladder. (line 4, col 5)

E-ERROR-006: `throw` is not a scrml keyword — §19 replaces it with `fail`. Declare the enclosing function as failable (`function name(...)! -> ErrorType`) and use `fail ErrorType::Variant(...)` to surface the error. (line 4, col 21)

E-ERROR-007: `try` is not a scrml keyword — §19 has no try/catch/finally. Handle failable calls with `!{ ::Variant(e) -> ... }`, the `?` propagation operator, or by matching the result enum. (line 3, col 5)

E-ERROR-007: `try` is not a scrml keyword — §19 has no try/catch/finally. Handle failable calls with `!{ ::Variant(e) -> ... }`, the `?` propagation operator, or by matching the result enum. (line 3, col 5)
E-LOOP-007: `while` is a statement, not an expression (§49.4.4). Use the `~` accumulator pattern to collect a value across iterations, or refactor to a `for/lift` expression.

E-LOOP-005: `break` inside an arrow function cannot target an outer loop (§49.4.3). Arrow functions are function boundaries; `break`/`continue` may only target loops declared in the same function scope. Move the control-flow out of the arrow body, or refactor the arrow into an inline loop.

E-LOOP-006: Label `L:` is defined more than once in the same scope (§49.2.2). Each loop label must be unique within its enclosing block. Rename one of the labels.

E-SYNTAX-002: `lift` is not valid inside a standard `function` body (§49.6.2). `lift` may only appear in logic blocks or component bodies. Return the value with `return` and have the caller lift it, or refactor the function into a component.

E-LIN-002: Linear variable `token` consumed inside a loop. Loop iteration count is unprovable; consume `token` before or after the loop. A 'lin' variable can only be used once. Clone it first, or change to 'const'/'let' if reuse is intended.

E-MU-001: Variable `y` was declared but never used before this scope closes. Either use the value somewhere (e.g., pass it to a function or reference it in a template), or prefix with `_` (e.g., `_y`) to suppress this warning, or remove the declaration.
E-EQ-001: cannot compare `number` with `bool` using `==` — scrml never coerces across types (§45). Convert one side explicitly (e.g. `toString`, `toNumber`) before comparing.

E-EQ-003: cannot compare values of struct type `Handler` with `==` — the struct contains a function-typed field, and functions have no structural equality (§45). Compare the specific data fields instead (e.g. `a.name == b.name`).

E-STRUCT-FUNCTION-FIELD: Struct field 'Handler.onFire' is declared with a function type ('( ) => void'). A function is not value data — it has no structural equality, is not serializable, and cannot be a map key — so it SHALL NOT be STORED as a struct field. (A function may be PASSED as a component prop or CALLED as an event handler, but never stored; §15.11.) For stored state, model the behavior as data (an enum tag the consumer matches on, or an engine). See SPEC §14.3 / §14.1.1.
E-EQ-002: `== not` is not valid — use `is not` to check for absence (§45).
E-SYNTAX-042: `null` is not a scrml token — scrml uses `not` for absence (§42). Replace `== null` with `x is not` (checks for absence) or `x is some` for presence.
E-EQ-004: `===` is not a valid scrml operator. Use `==` instead — scrml equality is always strict, so `==` is the only form (§45.7).
E-SYNTAX-042: `undefined` is not a scrml token — scrml uses `not` for absence (§42). Replace `== undefined` with `x is not` (checks for absence) or `x is some` for presence.

E-SCOPE-001: Undeclared identifier `undefined` in logic expression. No variable, function, type, or import with that name is in scope. Check for a typo, a missing `import`, or whether you meant a reactive `@undefined`.

E-SCOPE-001: Undeclared identifier `item` in logic expression. No variable, function, type, or import with that name is in scope. Check for a typo, a missing `import`, or whether you meant a reactive `@item`.
E-SYNTAX-044: `given` takes bare identifiers, not property paths (§42.2.3). Bind the property to a local variable first: `let n = u.<field>`, then `given n { ... }`. (line 5, col 11)
E-TYPE-062: Left-hand operand of `is` must be an enum-typed value, but `name` has type `string`. Use `name == someEnum.Admin` for non-enum comparisons, or annotate `name` with an enum type (§42).

E-MATCH-012: Match on `T | not` type lacks a `not` arm and lacks an `else`/wildcard arm. Add a `not => ...` arm or an `_ => ...` wildcard to handle the absence case (§42).
E-TYPE-006: Non-exhaustive match over union type. Missing members: string. Add arms for the missing types, or add an `else` arm to handle them all.

E-CG-003: match expression `match name { ... }` has no arm the code generator can lower — every arm failed to parse (unsupported arm shape). Rewrite the arms using a documented form (`.Variant => result`, `.Variant(binding) => result`, `"string" => result`, `else => result`; the `->` / `:>` separators are accepted aliases per SPEC §18.2).
E-EQ-004: `!==` is not a valid scrml operator. Use `!=` instead — scrml equality is always strict, so `!=` is the only form (§45.7).
E-TYPE-041: Cannot assign `not` to variable `x` of type `string`. Declare the type as `string | not` to allow absence values (§42).

E-TYPE-045: prefix `not` is not valid as boolean negation — `not` is the unified absence value, not a logical-negation operator. Use `!expr` (bare) or `!(expr)` (parenthesized) for boolean negation, or `expr is not` to check for absence (§42).

E-STRUCT-FUNCTION-FIELD: Struct field 'Handler.onFire' is declared with a function type ('( ) => void'). A function is not value data — it has no structural equality, is not serializable, and cannot be a map key — so it SHALL NOT be STORED as a struct field. (A function may be PASSED as a component prop or CALLED as an event handler, but never stored; §15.11.) For stored state, model the behavior as data (an enum tag the consumer matches on, or an engine). See SPEC §14.3 / §14.1.1.

E-ATTR-001: The value `get` is not valid for attribute `label`. Attribute values must be a quoted string literal, an unquoted identifier, or a call expression. For example: `label="hello"`, `label=myVar`, or `label=${expression}`. (line 9, col 43)

E-SCOPE-001: Undeclared identifier `label` in logic expression. No variable, function, type, or import with that name is in scope. Check for a typo, a missing `import`, or whether you meant a reactive `@label`.
E-ATTR-010: `bind:value` requires a reactive `@` variable or state field path. `name` is not reactive. Use `@name` or a state field path like `stateObj.field`. (line 4, col 24)

E-CONTROL-FLOW-IN-MARKUP: a bare `for` control-flow statement directly in a markup body is not recognised as logic and would ship as raw text into the DOM. Per §17.4 / §7, control flow in a markup body MUST be wrapped in a `${ ... }` logic block. Canonical iteration: `<ul>${ for (x of @items) { lift <li>${x}</> } }</>` (Tier-0, §17.4); or the Tier-1 `<each in=@items>` form (§17.7). (line 25, col 5)

E-CHANNEL-SHARED-MODIFIER: `@shared total = …` uses the `@shared` modifier, which is removed in v0.next (M19). Reactive cells declared inside a channel body auto-sync by virtue of being declared in the channel body — no marker is required. Remove the `@shared` keyword and use the V5-strict structural form `<total> = init` (inside a `<channel>` body) or a plain `<total> = init` / `@total = init` (outside a channel). (SPEC §38.4 + §34.)

E-META-001: Runtime variable 'bun' cannot be used inside meta context ^{}. Meta contexts execute at compile time and cannot access runtime values. Hint: Read all values at compile time, or move this logic outside ^{}.

E-META-001: JS-host ambient global 'bun' is not available inside ^{} meta blocks. Per SPEC §22.12 (Approach C), only scrml-native and the enumerated meta primitive set (reflect / emit / emit.raw / meta.*) are in scope. Hint: this surface is not available inside ^{} bodies.

E-COMPONENT-021: Component `Card` has a malformed body that could not be re-parsed. Phase 1 supports self-closing and simple open-tag component definitions only. Parse error: unexpected token in expression position: Ellipsis

E-COMPONENT-035: Component `Card` survived component expansion (CE) but was not resolved. This is a post-CE invariant violation: every markup node resolved to user-component (or unresolved with an uppercase tag) MUST be expanded into HTML markup or rejected with E-COMPONENT-020 at CE time. The residual reference would otherwise be silently emitted as `document.createElement("Card")`, producing a phantom DOM element with no content. Likely cause: cross-file component import is not yet supported in this consumption shape (see F-COMPONENT-001 deep-dive). Workaround: wrap the component call in an HTML element inside a `lift` expression, e.g. `lift
`.
